title: Course Schedule
problem_statement: There are a total of numCourses courses you have to take, labeled from 0 to numCourses - 1. You are given an array prerequisites where prerequisites[i] = [ai, bi] indicates that you must take course bi first if you want to take course ai. For example, the pair [0, 1], indicates that to take course 0 you have to first take course 1. Return true if you can finish all courses. Otherwise, return false.

examples.explanation: There are a total of 2 courses to take. To take course 1 you should have finished course 0. So it is possible.
There are a total of 2 courses to take. To take course 1 you should have finished course 0, and to take course 0 you should also have finished course 1. So it is impossible.

constraints: All the pairs prerequisites[i] are unique.
intuition: Model courses as nodes and prerequisites as directed edges. A cycle in the graph means it's impossible to finish all courses.

approaches.name: DFS Cycle Detection
approaches.description: Build the graph, then DFS from each node. Use a visited array: 0=not visited, 1=visiting, 2=visited. If hit 1 again, cycle.
approaches.why_not: BFS (Kahn's) is also fine; DFS is recursive and straightforward.
interview_commentary: This is a classic cycle detection problem. I'll model it as a directed graph where an edge from b to a means b is prerequisite for a.
DFS with three states: not visited (0), visiting (1), visited (2). If I encounter a node that's visiting (1), there's a cycle.
This visits each node and edge once, so O(V + E) time.

approaches.name: BFS Topological Sort (Kahn's Algorithm)
approaches.description: Use indegree array. Start with nodes with indegree 0. Process in queue, reduce indegrees. If all processed, no cycle.
approaches.why_not: Same complexity as DFS; BFS is iterative and good for large graphs.
interview_commentary: Kahn's algorithm uses BFS for topological sort. Nodes with indegree 0 have no prerequisites, so start there.
As we process, reduce indegrees of neighbors. If all nodes are processed, no cycle.
This is also O(V + E) and iterative, avoiding recursion stack issues.

interview_qa.q: Why does a cycle mean impossible to finish?
interview_qa.a: A cycle means there's a circular dependency, so you can't order the courses without violating prerequisites.

interview_qa.q: What's the difference between DFS and BFS for cycle detection?
interview_qa.a: DFS uses recursion and a visiting state to detect back edges. BFS uses indegrees and processes level by level.

interview_qa.q: Can we use Union-Find for this?
interview_qa.a: Union-Find detects undirected cycles, but here the graph is directed, so no.

interview_qa.q: What if there are multiple components?
interview_qa.a: Both DFS and BFS handle disconnected graphs by starting from each unvisited node.
